specification.topic-statement: The command history system maintains an ordered, deduplicated list of submitted entries and supports sequential backward/forward navigation through them.

scope.in-scope: In-memory storage of submitted command strings, capacity enforcement, deduplication and promotion rules, navigation cursor management, and pending input preservation during browsing.
scope.boundary: The history system is a pure in-memory structure. It does not read from or write to any persistent store. External code may populate an instance by submitting entries in sequence, and may read entries back for persistence by querying the current state.
The history system receives raw command strings for storage and returns stored strings during navigation. It does not interpret, validate, or transform the content of those strings in any way.

field.name: pending input
field.note: saved user input; absent until browsing begins, or after browsing is stopped or an entry is submitted

behavior.initialization: A history instance is created with a fixed maximum capacity. The entry list starts empty. The instance starts in the not-browsing state with no pending input saved.
behavior.submitting-an-entry:
A text string is submitted for storage. The submission proceeds through a fixed decision sequence.
If the submitted string is empty, it is silently discarded. No state is changed.
If the submitted string is identical to the most recently stored entry, the string is not added to the list. The navigation cursor resets to the not-browsing position and any saved pending input is discarded.
Submitting a duplicate of the most recent entry modifies browsing state and discards pending input even though nothing is added to the list. A caller mid-browse will silently lose their position and saved pending input.
If the submitted string matches any stored entry other than the most recent one, that existing entry is removed from its current position. All entries that were after it shift one position toward the beginning to close the gap. The submitted string is then appended as the new most recent entry. The count remains unchanged net (one removed, one added).
If the submitted string does not exist anywhere in the list and the list has reached its capacity, the oldest entry (the one at the beginning of the list) is removed. All remaining entries shift one position toward the beginning. The submitted string is then appended as the new most recent entry. The count remains unchanged net (one removed, one added).
If the submitted string does not exist anywhere in the list and the list is below capacity, the string is appended as the new most recent entry. The count increases by one.
After any path that modifies the list (existing-entry-promotion, capacity-enforcement, or normal-addition), the navigation cursor is reset to the not-browsing position and any saved pending input is discarded.
behavior.navigating-history:
Navigation moves a cursor through the stored entries. The cursor exists in one of three distinct positions:
- The browsing position: pointing at one of the stored entries. Browsing status is true only in this position. - The end position: one step past the last stored entry. Browsing status is false. This is the starting position of a newly created instance and the position returned to after stopping browsing or submitting an entry. - The past-end position: one step beyond the end position. Browsing status is false. Reached only by navigating forward from the end position.
Browsing is initiated by providing the current pending input string. Any previously saved pending input is discarded and replaced with the provided value.
If the entry list is empty, the pending input is saved but the cursor remains at the end position. The browsing status stays false.
If the entry list is not empty, the pending input is saved and the cursor moves to the most recent entry (the last position in the browsing range). Browsing status becomes true.
Initiating browsing when the cursor is already in the browsing position or the past-end position discards any previously saved pending input, saves the newly provided value, and unconditionally resets the cursor to the most recent entry.
When the entry list is empty, initiating browsing saves the pending input but does not change the browsing status to true. The instance is technically not browsing even though pending input has been preserved. This is indistinguishable from a case where pending input was saved by a previous browsing session that was not explicitly stopped.
A backward navigation step moves the cursor one position toward the oldest entry and returns the entry now pointed to.
If the cursor is at the oldest entry (the beginning of the list), nothing is returned and the cursor does not move.
If the cursor is at the end position or the past-end position (not browsing), nothing is returned and the cursor does not move.
A forward navigation step behaves differently depending on the current cursor position.
If the cursor is in the browsing position and not at the most recent entry: the cursor advances one position toward the most recent entry and returns the entry now pointed to.
If the cursor is in the browsing position at the most recent entry: the cursor advances to the end position and returns the saved pending input. The browsing status becomes false.
If the cursor is at the end position: the cursor advances to the past-end position and nothing is returned.
If the cursor is at the past-end position: nothing is returned and the cursor does not move.
Navigating forward from the end position advances to the past-end position without returning anything. Since both positions report the same not-browsing status and both return the same pending input via get-current, the distinction is invisible except through its effect on subsequent navigation. A caller that navigates forward once from the end position and then tries to navigate backward will receive nothing, because backward navigation checks browsing status rather than cursor position.
From the past-end position, both forward and backward navigation return nothing and do not move the cursor. The only exits from the past-end position are: initiating browsing again (which resets to the most recent entry) or submitting an entry (which resets to the end position and discards pending input). The saved pending input remains accessible via get-current while in the past-end position.
Browsing is explicitly stopped by resetting the cursor to the end position and discarding the saved pending input. After stopping, the browsing status is false and get-current returns nothing.
The entry at the current cursor position is readable without moving the cursor.
If the cursor is in the browsing position, the entry at that position is returned.
If the cursor is at the end position or past-end position, the saved pending input is returned. If no pending input has been saved, or if it was discarded by stopping browsing or submitting an entry, nothing is returned.
The browsing status is true only when the cursor is in the browsing position (pointing at a stored entry). It is false when the cursor is at the end position or the past-end position.
